steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence — Quantitative question. The researchers claim that juvenile plants gain an advantage by growing near other plants (in patches). The key baseline is that random distribution would put only ~15% of plants in patches; so supporting the claim requires data showing each species has a substantially higher percentage of juveniles in patches than that 15% baseline.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘For all five species, less than 75% of juvenile plants were growing in patches of vegetation.’: Saying fewer than 75% were in patches is uninformative without comparing to the 15% baseline — it could be consistent with the claim or could actually contradict it. It does not constitute support for an advantage. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The species with the greatest number of juvenile plants growing in patches of vegetation was H. stoechas.’: Focuses on only one species and compares species to each other rather than to the 15% expected under random distribution. Even if true, it does not show all five species benefit from patches. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘For T. libanitis and T. moroderi, the percentage of juvenile plants growing in patches was less than what would be expected if plants were randomly distributed.’: Claims these species had fewer juveniles in patches than the 15% random baseline — which would actively contradict the advantage claim, not support it. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘For each species, the percentage of juvenile plants growing in patches of vegetation was substantially higher than what would be expected if plants were randomly distributed.’: Directly compares all five species to the 15% random baseline and shows all exceed it, demonstrating a consistent advantage for juveniles growing in patches across every species studied. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
